spec_match.rationale: Cleanly visualises the partitioned `(top-level site, resource URL)` tuple vs the new `(SHARED, resource URL)` tuple with allowlist matching, which is exactly the keying change the feature introduces.

interactive.rationale: Real controls (top-site selector, freeform resource URL field, presets, add/reset) build a live grid of cache-key cards and update three live counters — well past the static-card bar.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Blurb promises a side-by-side of the pre-146 partitioned key vs the new shared key, and the explorer delivers exactly that tuple comparison plus a saved-fetches tally.

edge_cases.rationale: Allowlist mismatch (the private-script preset) and re-fetch hits are covered, but no scenarios for query-string variation, hash mismatch, allowlist eviction, third-party cookies in the request, or HTTPS-to-HTTP downgrade that would disqualify a URL.

references.rationale: The see-also section links to https://chromestatus.com/feature/5202380930678784 and the pervasive-payloads explainer.

design_system.rationale: All colours from CSS variables, badges use accent-emerald/text-black with paired backgrounds and explicit foregrounds, no static-card pattern.

openQuestions.title: Add disqualifying-edge presets
openQuestions.detail: The explorer covers the happy match and the obvious miss, but the interesting failure modes are URL-shape-driven: same script with a tracking query parameter (?u=...), different version path, different protocol, mixed-content downgrade. Add three or four 'looks pervasive but disqualifies' presets so authors learn the boundary, not just the centre.

openQuestions.title: Show what the partition actually keys on
openQuestions.detail: The current 'site' label is a hand-rolled `host.split('.').slice(-2)` which is wrong for eTLD+1 (e.g. `co.uk`). Either use a real PSL lookup, or document explicitly that the demo is illustrative and link out to the Network Partition Key spec definition.

openQuestions.title: Expose the allowlist update cadence
openQuestions.detail: Authors will ask 'how do I know if my CDN URL is on the list?'. Add a small panel or footnote describing how Chrome updates the allowlist (component update, milestone-bound, etc.) and link to the upstream allowlist source if one exists.

summary: Strongest concept in this feature folder — actually surfaces the cache-key tuple, which is the only thing that visibly changes. Polish the edges (query-string disqualifiers, real eTLD+1, allowlist provenance) and it becomes a definitive reference.
